title: Home battery storage payback explained
description: How long a home battery takes to pay for itself in the UK: real installed costs, savings per kWh, worked payback examples and the wear cost most guides skip.
blurb: Real UK payback maths for home batteries: installed costs, savings per kWh cycled, and honest timescales against the warranty.
lede: A home battery is one of the few energy upgrades where the sales pitch and the spreadsheet regularly disagree. Installers quote paybacks of six or seven years; independent maths usually lands closer to ten or more. The truth depends almost entirely on three numbers: what the battery costs you per usable kilowatt hour, the gap between your cheapest and most expensive electricity rates, and how hard you cycle it. This guide works through all three with current UK prices, so you can run the sums for your own house rather than trusting a quote.
short_answer: A 10 kWh home battery costs roughly £6,000 to £7,500 installed and typically saves £450 to £750 a year when paired with a cheap overnight tariff, giving a simple payback of about 9 to 14 years. That sits uncomfortably close to the usual 10 to 12 year warranty. Payback only beats the warranty if you cycle the battery hard against a big spread between your cheap and peak rates, which is why the tariff matters as much as the hardware.

sections.h2: What a home battery actually costs in 2026
sections.html:
Installed prices have fallen steadily, but a battery is still a four-figure purchase. Expect to pay roughly £500 to £900 per usable kilowatt hour installed, with bigger systems cheaper per unit of storage.

Usable capacityTypical installed costCost per kWh of storage
5 kWh£3,500 to £4,500£700 to £900
10 kWh£6,000 to £7,500£600 to £750
13.5 kWh£8,000 to £9,500£590 to £700

Typical UK installed prices, mid 2026, including 0% VAT. Prices vary by brand, location and installation complexity, so always compare several quotes.

Three things move the price. Installing a battery at the same time as solar panels is cheaper than retrofitting one later, because the labour and electrical work are shared. A hybrid inverter that handles both panels and battery costs less than adding a separate battery inverter to an existing system. And battery storage currently qualifies for 0% VAT, including standalone retrofits without solar, until 31 March 2027, after which the rate is due to revert to 5%. Schemes and tax reliefs change, so check the current rules on the official GOV.UK energy-saving materials page before you sign anything.

sections.h2: The three ways a battery earns its keep
sections.html:
A battery does not generate anything. It only ever moves electricity from a cheap moment to an expensive one, and every route has a value per kilowatt hour you can calculate.

1. Storing solar you would otherwise export

Without a battery, surplus solar goes to the grid for a Smart Export Guarantee payment of roughly 5p to 15p per kWh. Stored and used at home instead, that energy displaces grid electricity at 26.11p. After round-trip losses of about 10%, each stored kilowatt hour is worth the avoided import minus the export payment you gave up: roughly 8p to 19p depending on your SEG rate. Notice the catch: the better your export rate, the less a battery adds to a solar system.

2. Charging cheap overnight, using it at peak rates

On a time-of-use tariff with a night rate around 7p (some EV tariffs are nearer 5.5p), the battery charges cheaply and runs the house during the day instead of buying at 26.11p. After losses, that saves about 18p to 20p per kWh shifted. This is currently the strongest earner, and it works with or without panels; we cover the mechanics in detail in our guide to battery arbitrage on a cheap night tariff.

3. Exporting at peak times

Some smart tariffs pay more for export in the evening peak than charging costs overnight. Buying at 7p and selling at 15p or more nets a few pence per kWh after losses. It is a useful top-up for larger batteries rather than a payback engine on its own.

Here are four honest scenarios. Each assumes the battery is well used: cycled most days, sized so the household can actually absorb what it stores.

SetupInstalled costRealistic annual savingSimple payback
5 kWh, solar only, flat-rate tariff£4,000£110 to £24017 to 36 years
5 kWh, cheap night rate, no solar£4,000£300 to £36511 to 13 years
10 kWh, night rate plus 4 kW solar£6,750£500 to £70010 to 14 years
13.5 kWh, night rate, solar and peak export£9,000£700 to £9509 to 13 years

At 26.11p per kWh peak (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026), 5.5p to 7p off-peak EV-tariff rates, SEG export 5p to 15p, and 90% round-trip efficiency. Savings assume near-daily cycling.

The first row is the one glossy brochures skip. A small battery bolted onto solar while staying on a flat tariff often never pays for itself, because in winter there is little surplus to store and in summer the export payment you sacrifice eats much of the gain. The pattern across the table is clear: the tariff does more work than the panels. That is also why a battery can make sense with no solar at all, a case we test properly in is a home battery without solar worth it, and why the battery question is really a sub-question of whether solar stacks up for your house in the first place.

sections.h2: The wear cost most payback calculators ignore
sections.html:
Every cycle consumes a little of the battery's life, and that wear has a price. Take the 10 kWh example: £6,750 spread over a typical 6,000-cycle warranty at 90% efficiency is about 54,000 useful kilowatt hours, or roughly 12.5p of battery wear per kWh delivered.

Run the honest sum with that included. Charging at 7p plus 12.5p of wear gives a true cost of about 19.5p per kWh, against 26.11p from the grid. The battery still wins, but the real economic margin is around 7p per kWh, not the 19p a simple comparison suggests. Two consequences follow:

  • A battery that sits half-idle is losing money. Lithium cells age with the calendar as well as with use, so a lightly cycled battery wears out almost as fast while earning far less. Cycle it hard.
  • Small spreads are not worth chasing. Shifting energy to save 5p per kWh costs more in wear than it returns. The strategy only works against a large cheap-to-peak gap.

The flip side is that warranties are conservative. Many batteries keep working well past 6,000 cycles at reduced capacity, and every year of life beyond the warranty pushes the wear cost down and the effective return up.

If you only check five things before buying, make it these:

  • Your tariff spread. The gap between cheap and peak rates is the single biggest driver. A 20p spread roughly doubles the earnings of a 10p spread for the same hardware.
  • How much electricity you use at peak times. The battery can only save money on consumption it displaces. A household using 6 kWh a day cannot make a 13.5 kWh battery pay; a 12 kWh-a-day household can.
  • Battery size matched to daily use. Size the battery to the peak-rate consumption you can realistically shift, not to the biggest unit the installer stocks. Oversizing stretches payback badly.
  • Your export rate if you have solar. On a 15p SEG rate the battery adds little to summer solar economics; most of its value then comes from winter night-rate charging. Our solar battery storage guide covers how to weigh storage against simply exporting.
  • Electricity prices themselves. Every payback figure on this page is anchored to today's cap. If peak prices rise faster than off-peak, paybacks shorten; if the spread narrows, they stretch.

One thing deliberately missing from the list: backup power. Riding through a power cut is a genuine benefit, but only if your system is specified for it (many standard installs shut down with the grid), and it is a comfort feature, not a saving. Buy it knowingly rather than letting a salesperson count it as money.

At mid-2026 prices, a well-sized home battery on a strong time-of-use tariff pays back in roughly 9 to 14 years, against a typical 10 to 12 year warranty. As a pure investment it is marginal: the maths works, but only just, and only if you cycle the battery hard against a wide tariff spread. It clearly suits high-consumption households (heat pumps, EVs, home workers) on EV or smart tariffs, and homeowners adding a battery alongside new solar where the marginal install cost is lower. It clearly does not suit low-usage homes, flat-rate tariffs, or anyone hoping a small battery will transform a solar system that already earns a good export rate.

Before you commit, do three things. Get your last twelve months of consumption and work out how many peak-rate kilowatt hours per day you could actually shift. Price the tariff change as well as the hardware, because the tariff is half the machine. And get at least three quotes while the 0% VAT window lasts, checking the current rules on GOV.UK, since reliefs and export rates both change.

faq.q: How long does a home battery last?
faq.a: Most lithium home batteries carry a 10 to 12 year warranty, or 6,000 to 10,000 cycles, with a promise of around 70% of original capacity remaining at the end. In practice many keep working beyond the warranty at reduced capacity, which improves the lifetime economics.

faq.q: Can a battery pay for itself without solar panels?
faq.a: Yes. Charging overnight at 5.5p to 7p on an EV or time-of-use tariff and using that energy instead of 26.11p peak-rate electricity saves around 18p to 20p per kWh shifted. For many households this earns more per pound spent than using a battery purely to store solar, especially if their solar export rate is already good.

faq.q: Is there any grant or VAT relief for home batteries?
faq.a: There is no general cash grant, but battery storage qualifies for 0% VAT in Great Britain until 31 March 2027, including standalone batteries fitted without solar. The rate is due to revert to 5% after that. Check the current rules on the official GOV.UK energy-saving materials page, as reliefs change.

faq.q: Will a home battery keep the lights on in a power cut?
faq.a: Only if the system is specified with backup capability. Many standard grid-tied installations shut down automatically when the grid fails, for safety. Backup adds cost and usually covers selected circuits rather than the whole house, so confirm exactly what your quote includes.

faq.q: What size battery should I buy?
faq.a: Match the battery to the peak-rate electricity you can realistically shift each day, not to your total consumption. A household using 10 to 12 kWh a day, mostly outside cheap hours, suits an 8 to 10 kWh battery. Buying more capacity than you can cycle most days stretches the payback significantly.
